These mountains have humbled experienced trampers for over a century, with weather that can change from calm to deadly in minutes. The ridgeline between the major peaks is fully exposed to the winds that howl through Cook Strait, and windchill can drop temperatures to well below freezing at any time of year. But for those with the experience and preparation to tackle them safely, the Tararuas offer a genuine alpine wilderness experience just an hour from Wellington. The route to Mitre Peak at 1,571 metres is a classic, climbing through dense bush to the tussock tops before following an exposed ridge to the summit. DOC huts along the main routes provide shelter and are maintained to a good standard. Check the weather forecast obsessively, carry a PLB, leave your intentions, and respect the mountains. These ranges do not forgive complacency.

reviews.summary: The Tararua Range Tramping adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Tramp the rugged Tararua Ranges, the North Island's most challenging alpine tramping. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Mokai Gravity Canyon (Rangitikei)
pois.short: Bungy, swing, or fly fox from 80 metres above the spectacular Rangitikei Gorge.
pois.commentary: Mokai Gravity Canyon offers a bungy jump, bridge swing, and flying fox from 80 metres above the Rangitikei River gorge. The North Island's highest bungy, the swing reaches speeds of 150 km/h, and the flying fox runs for 1 km across the gorge. The setting in the dramatic Rangitikei canyon is spectacular.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at Mokai Gravity Canyon, perched 80 metres above the dramatic Rangitikei River gorge, and this is where the North Island's biggest thrills await. Three different ways to throw yourself off a perfectly good platform are on offer here. The bungy jump is the North Island's highest, an 80-metre freefall towards the river below with a rebound that sends you soaring back upwards. The bridge swing is even more intense, you freefall for 50 metres before swinging through a massive arc at up to 150 kilometres per hour. And the flying fox, the longest in New Zealand, carries you on a one-kilometre cable across the entire gorge at speeds up to 160 kilometres per hour. The Rangitikei Gorge itself is a geological masterpiece, layers of ancient river sediment cut into a deep, narrow canyon by the river below. This is one of those rare adventure sites where the natural setting is as impressive as the activities themselves.

reviews.summary: The Mokai Gravity Canyon (Rangitikei) adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Bungy, swing, or fly fox from 80 metres above the spectacular Rangitikei Gorge. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Mangatepopo / Humphries Castle Rock Climbing
pois.short: Climb volcanic rock at the Manawatu Gorge area crags popular with local climbers.
pois.commentary: The Manawatu region has several rock climbing areas, including crags near the Manawatu Gorge and in the Tararua Range foothills. The rock is varied, from greywacke to volcanic, and routes range from beginner to advanced. The climbing community is active and welcoming.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at one of the Manawatu region's outdoor rock climbing areas, where local climbers have developed routes on the varied rock formations of the Tararua foothills and gorge country. The climbing here is not as well known as Wharepapa South or Castle Hill, but that is part of its appeal, you are unlikely to queue for a route, and the local community is welcoming to visitors. The rock types vary from compact greywacke to volcanic formations, offering different styles of climbing from friction slabs to steep crack lines. Routes range from introductory climbs suitable for guided beginners to challenging pitches that will test experienced leaders. The setting is beautiful, surrounded by farmland and native bush, with the Tararua Ranges as a backdrop. Bring your own gear or connect with the local climbing club, who can point you to the best routes for your ability and the current conditions. This is grassroots New Zealand climbing at its most authentic.

reviews.summary: The Mangatepopo / Humphries Castle Rock Climbing adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Climb volcanic rock at the Manawatu Gorge area crags popular with local climbers. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Himatangi Beach Horse Trekking
pois.short: Ride horses along the wild Manawatu coastline at Himatangi Beach.
pois.commentary: Horse trekking at Himatangi Beach takes riders along the long, flat stretch of black sand on the Manawatu coast. The wide, empty beach provides room for cantering, and the dramatic Tasman Sea backdrop makes for a memorable ride. Suitable for all experience levels.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at Himatangi Beach on the Manawatu coast, where the Tasman Sea meets a long stretch of dark sand and a horse is waiting to carry you along it. This is a classic New Zealand beach ride, wide open space, the crash of surf on one side, and the freedom to canter along the waterline with salt spray on your face. The Manawatu coast is wild and largely undeveloped, and you are likely to have the beach mostly to yourselves. Your guide will match you with a suitable horse and give you a thorough briefing before heading down to the sand. Beginners walk and trot along the firmer sand near the waterline, while experienced riders may get the chance to open up to a canter along the straightaways. The beach seems to stretch endlessly in both directions, and the sense of space is liberating. After your ride, the horses deserve the credit, they are calm, experienced beach mounts who know the terrain by heart.

reviews.summary: The Himatangi Beach Horse Trekking adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Ride horses along the wild Manawatu coastline at Himatangi Beach. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Brew Union Brewing Co
pois.short: Palmerston North's microbrewery and eatery — house-brewed beers alongside NZ guest taps.
pois.commentary: Brew Union has brought quality craft beer to Palmerston North with a family-friendly microbrewery, bar and eatery. Alongside fresh house-brewed beers, there's a changing selection from independent breweries and cider houses across the country. The food menu is designed to pair with the beers, and the atmosphere is relaxed and welcoming. A bright spot in the Manawatū drinking scene.

reviews.summary: A welcome addition to Palmerston North. The house beers are solid and improving. Some find the food inconsistent.

pois.name: Café Cuba
pois.short: Downtown Palmerston North institution — excellent baked goods, good coffee and a loyal local following.
pois.commentary: Café Cuba has been a Palmerston North favourite for years, known for its impressive cabinet of baked goods, cakes and slices. The coffee is consistently good, and the brunch menu covers all the bases. It's the kind of reliable neighbourhood café that every town needs — unpretentious, welcoming and always busy. The cheese scones are legendary among regulars.

reviews.summary: A Palmy institution. The baked goods are the star attraction. Nothing flashy, just consistently good café food.

pois.name: Roots Brewing Co
pois.short: Whanganui craft brewery — locally sourced ingredients and small-batch beers with character.
pois.commentary: Roots Brewing Co in Whanganui sources local ingredients wherever possible, creating beers with genuine regional character. The small-batch approach means the range changes frequently, and there's always something interesting on tap. The brewery is part of Whanganui's growing creative scene, and the taproom has become a gathering spot for the town's artists, musicians and food lovers.

reviews.summary: Creative beers with local character. The taproom atmosphere is welcoming. Small operation means limited hours.

pois.name: The Yellow House Restaurant
pois.short: Whanganui's top restaurant — seasonal NZ cuisine in a heritage villa on the riverbank.
pois.commentary: The Yellow House occupies a beautiful heritage villa on Whanganui's riverbank, serving seasonal New Zealand cuisine with a strong focus on local produce. The menu changes regularly and features wild game, river fish and vegetables from the rich Whanganui soil. The intimate setting — just a few tables in the villa's dining rooms — makes it feel like eating at a very talented friend's house. Booking essential.

reviews.summary: Whanganui's best dining experience. The heritage villa setting is charming. Small capacity means booking ahead is essential.

pois.name: Feilding Farmers' Market & Saleyards
pois.short: NZ's most beautiful town (self-proclaimed) — Friday market and the last remaining livestock saleyards.
pois.commentary: Feilding's Friday morning farmers' market is a showcase for Manawatū produce — the rich farmland produces exceptional dairy, meat and vegetables. The market runs alongside Feilding's famous livestock saleyards, the last in New Zealand still operating at scale. Watching the cattle auction is a uniquely NZ rural experience. The town itself is charming, with heritage buildings and good cafés on Manchester Street.

reviews.summary: A genuine rural NZ experience. The market produce is excellent. The saleyards are fascinating if you're interested in farming culture.

pois.name: Ruapehu Brewing Co
pois.short: Ohakune's craft brewery — small-batch beers perfect for après-ski or after a Tongariro Crossing.
pois.commentary: Ruapehu Brewing Co in Ohakune produces small-batch beers inspired by the mountains. The taproom is popular with skiers, trampers and mountain bikers — basically anyone who's earned a cold beer after a day of activity. The Alpine Pale Ale and the seasonal Carrot Stout (Ohakune is the carrot capital of NZ) are favourites. The relaxed atmosphere and honest prices make it a welcome stop.

reviews.summary: Perfect après-adventure beer. The Carrot Stout is a fun local novelty. Small operation with limited hours outside ski season.

pois.name: Eat at the Junction (Ohakune)
pois.short: Ohakune's best restaurant — seasonal NZ cuisine after skiing, tramping or mountain biking.
pois.commentary: Eat at the Junction has brought quality seasonal dining to Ohakune, the adventure gateway to Tongariro and Turoa. The menu changes with the seasons — hearty game and root vegetable dishes in winter, lighter fare in summer — and the wine list features small NZ producers. It's where skiers and trampers converge for a proper dinner, and the quality is a pleasant surprise in a small mountain town.

reviews.summary: The best food in Ohakune. Seasonal menus keep it interesting year-round. Hours can be limited outside ski season.

pois.name: Whanganui River Road Farm Shops
pois.short: Honesty-box farm stalls along the scenic Whanganui River Road — seasonal produce from riverside farms.
pois.commentary: The Whanganui River Road (a scenic drive from Whanganui to Pipiriki) passes through remote farming country where honesty-box farm stalls appear at gateways. Seasonal produce — feijoas, apples, kūmara, free-range eggs, honey — is left out with a price and a money box. It's a distinctly New Zealand food experience that relies on trust and the honour system. Bring cash and enjoy one of the country's most scenic drives.

reviews.summary: A charming, only-in-NZ food experience. The produce is fresh and cheap. The honour system works because it's that kind of community.

pois.name: Bushy Park Tarapuruhi — Predator-Free Sanctuary
pois.short: Predator-fenced sanctuary near Whanganui — NI robin, hihi, and kaka in magnificent old-growth bush.
pois.commentary: Bushy Park Tarapuruhi near Whanganui is a predator-fenced sanctuary protecting 87 hectares of old-growth lowland forest. North Island robin, hihi (stitchbird), kaka, and whitehead have been translocated here. The ancient rata, rimu, and kahikatea trees are magnificent.

reviews.summary: Outstanding old-growth forest. The birds are thriving. Easy walking tracks. A real conservation success.

pois.name: Manawatū Estuary — Shorebird Roost
pois.short: Internationally significant shorebird roost — bar-tailed godwit, wrybill, and banded dotterel.
pois.commentary: The Manawatū River Estuary at Foxton Beach is one of the most important shorebird sites in the lower North Island. Bar-tailed godwit, wrybill, banded dotterel, and red knot use the estuary during migration. The Manawatū Estuary Trust runs guided walks and monitoring.

reviews.summary: Excellent wader birding. The trust provides useful information. Low tide is essential. Migration season is best.

pois.name: Rangitatau Reserve — Bush Bird Walk
pois.short: Whanganui's urban bush reserve with tui, kereru, and fantail on easy walking tracks.
pois.commentary: Rangitatau Reserve on the edge of Whanganui city offers easy walking tracks through native bush. Community pest control has boosted bird populations, with good numbers of tui, kereru, fantail, and grey warbler. The reserve provides a pleasant bush experience close to town.

reviews.summary: Nice urban bush walk. Good bird variety for a city reserve. Easy tracks suitable for families.

pois.name: Whanganui River — Giant Longfin Eels
pois.short: Giant longfin eels in the Whanganui River — culturally significant tuna in a river with legal personhood.
pois.commentary: The Whanganui River, which has legal personhood under NZ law (Te Awa Tupua), is home to giant longfin eels (tuna). These ancient fish are culturally significant to local iwi. Viewing spots along the lower river allow observation of large eels in clear pools.

reviews.summary: Fascinating combination of ecology and law. The eels are impressive. The river's legal status adds depth.

pois.name: Pukaha Mount Bruce — National Wildlife Centre
pois.short: National wildlife centre with kōkako, kiwi, tuatara, and the famous white kiwi — Manukura's legacy.
pois.commentary: Pukaha Mount Bruce is a national wildlife centre and breeding facility for NZ's rarest species. The centre is famous as the birthplace of Manukura, the white kiwi. Kōkako, Campbell Island teal, shore plover, and tuatara are all housed here. The surrounding bush has been pest-controlled for decades.

reviews.summary: Excellent conservation centre. The kokako are a highlight. Good bush walks around the centre. Family-friendly.

pois.name: Rangitikei River Gorge — NZ Falcon Habitat
pois.short: Dramatic river gorge where NZ falcon (kārearea) hunt from the cliff faces.
pois.commentary: The Rangitikei River Gorge provides dramatic cliff nesting habitat for NZ falcon (kārearea). The falcons hunt from cliff-top perches, stooping on prey at high speed. The gorge scenery is spectacular, with the river cutting through papa rock. Several viewpoints along the road.

reviews.summary: Dramatic scenery. Falcon sightings require patience. The gorge itself is impressive. Good from roadside viewpoints.

pois.name: Kāpiti Marine Reserve
pois.short: Marine reserve around Kāpiti Island — protected reef systems with blue cod, crayfish, and kelp forests.
pois.commentary: The Kāpiti Marine Reserve protects the waters around Kāpiti Island, one of the North Island's most important nature reserves. Blue cod, crayfish, butterfish, and diverse reef communities thrive in the protected waters. Snorkelling and diving from the mainland shore or by boat.

reviews.summary: Excellent marine reserve. Fish life is visibly recovering. Best accessed with a local dive operator.

pois.name: Foxton River Loop — Wetland Birds
pois.short: Old river loop wetland with pūkeko, paradise shelduck, and seasonal wading birds near Foxton.
pois.commentary: The Foxton River Loop is a former course of the Manawatū River, now a productive wetland. Pūkeko, paradise shelduck, grey teal, and seasonal wading birds are easily observed from walking tracks. The de Molen windmill nearby adds a quirky Dutch-themed attraction.

reviews.summary: Pleasant wetland walk. Good bird variety. Combine with the de Molen windmill visit.

pois.name: Tōtara Reserve — Ancient Forest Birds
pois.short: Ancient tōtara forest with excellent bush birds — kererū, tūī, and fantail in towering old-growth trees.
pois.commentary: Tōtara Reserve in the Pohangina Valley protects a magnificent stand of ancient tōtara trees, some over 500 years old. The forest is alive with kererū, tūī, fantail, and grey warbler. The loop track is easy and well-maintained, passing through one of the best remaining lowland podocarp forests in the region.

reviews.summary: Magnificent old-growth forest. The giant tōtara trees are impressive. Easy walking. Good bush birding.

pois.name: Sarjeant Gallery Te Whare o Rehua
pois.short: Magnificent neoclassical gallery atop Pukenamu Queens Park, housing a nationally significant art collection.
pois.commentary: The Sarjeant Gallery Te Whare o Rehua is one of New Zealand's most architecturally distinguished galleries, a neoclassical building of Oamaru stone that crowns Pukenamu Queens Park Hill in central Whanganui. Opened in 1919 through a bequest from local farmer Henry Sarjeant, the gallery houses a nationally significant collection of over 10,000 works, with particular strengths in New Zealand photography, prints, and paintings. The building itself, with its grand portico and domed skylights, is as much a draw as the art within. The gallery underwent a major $68 million earthquake-strengthening and redevelopment project, creating additional exhibition space and modern facilities while preserving the historic building's grandeur. The collection includes important works by Edith Collier, a pioneering Whanganui-born modernist painter. The gallery shares Pukenamu with the Whanganui Regional Museum and war memorial, creating a cultural precinct with sweeping river views. Admission is free. A must-visit for art and architecture lovers.

reviews.summary: A stunning building housing a surprisingly strong art collection. The restoration is magnificent and the hilltop setting is dramatic. One of New Zealand's best regional galleries — free entry is a bonus.

pois.name: Royal Wanganui Opera House
pois.short: Magnificent 1899 opera house with a rare Wurlitzer organ, one of NZ's finest surviving Victorian theatres.
pois.commentary: The Royal Wanganui Opera House is one of New Zealand's most treasured heritage performing arts venues, opened in 1899 and magnificently preserved as an outstanding example of Victorian theatre architecture. The intimate 830-seat auditorium features ornate plasterwork, gilded details, and excellent acoustics. The building's greatest treasure is its 1932 Mighty Wurlitzer organ — one of the few remaining theatre pipe organs in the world still in its original location and in full working order. Regular organ recitals are held, and hearing the Wurlitzer fill the auditorium is a remarkable experience. The opera house hosts a year-round programme of touring shows, concerts, comedy, community performances, and events. The building has been listed as a Category I historic place by Heritage New Zealand and has received careful maintenance over its long life. Guided tours are available by arrangement. Located on St Hill Street in central Whanganui, the opera house is the centrepiece of a town that punches well above its weight culturally. Check the programme for Wurlitzer recitals during your visit.

reviews.summary: An absolutely beautiful heritage theatre — the Wurlitzer organ performances are unforgettable. The interior is stunning and intimate. One of New Zealand's finest theatres and a jewel of Whanganui.

pois.name: Ohakune Big Carrot
pois.short: Iconic giant carrot sculpture celebrating Ohakune's market garden heritage — a classic NZ 'Big Things' photo stop.
pois.commentary: The Ohakune Big Carrot is one of New Zealand's most beloved 'Big Things' — a seven-metre-tall carrot sculpture that has welcomed visitors to this mountain town since 1984. Ohakune's rich volcanic soils and market garden history, particularly its carrot-growing tradition established by Chinese settlers, are celebrated by this cheerful landmark. The carrot stands on the southern approach to town and is an obligatory photo stop for travellers heading to or from the Tongariro region. Ohakune itself is a dual-personality town — a ski resort hub in winter (gateway to Tūroa skifield on Mt Ruapehu) and an outdoor adventure base in summer. The annual Ohakune Carrot Carnival in June celebrates the humble root vegetable with carnival rides, competitions, and carrot-themed events. The Big Carrot has been repainted and refreshed several times but retains its original kitschy charm. Free to visit at any time, with a small parking area beside the sculpture. Just add it to the photo collection alongside the L&P bottle and the big trout.

reviews.summary: It's a giant carrot — what's not to love? A classic Kiwi road trip photo stop that always raises a smile. Quick stop but essential for the Big Things collection.

pois.name: Bulls Town Signs
pois.short: Small Manawatu town where every business uses bull-related puns on their signs — a delightfully Kiwi experience.
pois.commentary: Bulls is a small town on State Highway 1 in the Rangitikei district that has embraced its bovine name with wholehearted enthusiasm. Every business and public building in town features a bull-related pun on its signage — the police station is 'Const-a-Bull,' the medical centre is 'Cure-a-Bull,' the information centre is 'Inform-a-Bull,' the church is 'Worship-a-Bull,' and the toilets are 'Relieve-a-Bull.' The tradition has become so well-known that it draws visitors specifically to photograph the signs, and new businesses eagerly join in the punning tradition. The town embraces the concept with good humour and a touch of small-town pride. Bulls is a natural rest stop on the main highway between Wellington and the central North Island, and the signs give you a reason to slow down and explore the main street. A small museum in town provides local history context. The puns range from clever to groan-worthy, which is exactly the point. Free to explore — just park on the main street and walk. Quintessential small-town New Zealand.

reviews.summary: Absolutely love-a-bull! The town's commitment to the bull puns is charming and hilarious. A fun quick stop that always gets a laugh. Takes about 20 minutes to photograph them all.

pois.name: Castlecliff Beach
pois.short: Wild black-sand surf beach at the mouth of the Whanganui River with powerful waves and dramatic coastal scenery.
pois.commentary: Castlecliff Beach is Whanganui's principal ocean beach, a wild and dramatic stretch of black iron sand at the mouth of the Whanganui River on the Tasman Sea coast. The beach receives powerful westerly swells and offers challenging surf conditions popular with experienced surfers. The black sand, pounding waves, and often moody Tasman Sea skies create a distinctly raw and atmospheric coastal experience quite different from the golden sand beaches of the east coast. The beach extends for kilometres in both directions from the river mouth, offering plenty of space for walking, running, and beachcombing. Driftwood accumulates in dramatic piles along the shore. The area has strong historical connections — the river mouth was a significant point for Māori navigation and early European settlement. Swimming requires caution due to strong currents and the river mouth dynamics. The nearby Castlecliff suburb has a community surf club. The beach is easily accessed from Whanganui city, about 10 minutes' drive. Stunning in all weather conditions, particularly during dramatic westerly storms.

reviews.summary: A wild and beautiful beach with real drama — the black sand and pounding surf are impressive. Not for casual swimming but great for walking and surfing. Best in stormy weather for sheer atmosphere.

pois.name: Bridge to Nowhere Mountain Bike
pois.short: Remote mountain bike ride through the Whanganui backcountry to the iconic Bridge to Nowhere.
pois.commentary: The Bridge to Nowhere mountain bike track offers an extraordinary backcountry adventure deep in the Whanganui National Park. The ride combines jet boat access up the Whanganui River with a trail through regenerating bush to the Mangapurua Valley's iconic concrete bridge, built in 1936 to serve farming settlers who later abandoned the remote valley. The bridge now stands alone in the bush, a poignant monument to failed settlement. The mountain bike track follows the old road formation through the valley, passing abandoned farmsteads being reclaimed by native forest. The ride is moderately challenging with some technical sections. Several operators offer packages combining jet boat transport, bike hire, and guided or self-guided options, typically running as a day trip from Pipiriki or Whakahoro. The remote wilderness setting and historic story make this much more than a bike ride. Fitness and basic mountain biking skills are required.

reviews.summary: A truly unique New Zealand adventure combining history, wilderness, and mountain biking. The Bridge to Nowhere is hauntingly atmospheric. The jet boat ride up the Whanganui River is an adventure in itself.

pois.name: Patea Freezing Works Site
pois.short: Ruins of the Patea freezing works, an industrial heritage site and symbol of small-town economic change.
pois.commentary: The ruins of the Patea Cooperative Freezing Company works stand on the banks of the Patea River as a powerful reminder of the economic changes that transformed many small New Zealand towns. The freezing works operated from 1903 until its closure in 1982, at its peak employing around 1,000 workers and forming the economic backbone of the community. The closure devastated the town, inspiring the Patea Maori Club's internationally successful 1984 song 'Poi E.' Today the concrete and brick ruins are slowly being reclaimed by vegetation, creating a hauntingly photogenic industrial landscape. While the site is not formally developed as a heritage attraction, it is accessible and information about its history is available in the town. Patea itself is a small, resilient South Taranaki community on SH3 between Whanganui and Hawera. The site is a sobering reflection on industrial change in rural New Zealand.

reviews.summary: A poignant heritage site with a powerful story of community resilience. The ruins are atmospheric and photogenic. Not a polished tourist attraction, but deeply meaningful for those interested in New Zealand social history.

pois.name: Feilding Livestock Saleyards
pois.short: The Southern Hemisphere's largest livestock saleyards, where Friday sales showcase NZ's farming heartland.
pois.commentary: The Feilding Saleyards are the largest livestock auction yards in the Southern Hemisphere, handling hundreds of thousands of sheep and cattle annually. Friday sale days are a spectacle of rural New Zealand commerce, with farmers from across the Manawatu and beyond bringing stock to be auctioned in the covered yards. Visitors can observe the auctions from viewing platforms, watching the rapid-fire auctioneering and the practiced skills of stock handlers. A guided tour, available on Friday mornings, provides context for the auction process and the role of the saleyards in New Zealand's agricultural economy. The adjacent Feilding township was voted New Zealand's most beautiful town and has an attractive heritage main street. The Coach House Museum nearby houses local history collections. Feilding is 20 minutes north of Palmerston North on SH54. A genuine slice of agricultural New Zealand rarely seen by tourists.

reviews.summary: A fascinating insight into real New Zealand farming culture. The scale of the operation is impressive and the Friday auctions are riveting. Guides make the experience accessible to city visitors.

pois.name: Te Manawa Museum & Art Gallery
pois.short: Combined museum, art gallery, and science centre in Palmerston North with interactive exhibits and regional collections.
pois.commentary: Te Manawa is Palmerston North's combined museum, art gallery, and science centre, housed in a modern building on Main Street East. The museum section presents the natural and social history of the Manawatu region, with collections spanning Maori taonga, colonial settlement, and the area's rich agricultural heritage. The art gallery features a permanent collection of New Zealand art alongside rotating exhibitions. The science centre offers interactive exhibits that engage younger visitors with hands-on learning. The facility hosts regular temporary exhibitions of national significance. Te Manawa is free to visit, making it an accessible cultural experience for families and visitors. The museum is centrally located near the Square, Palmerston North's main public space, with cafes and shopping nearby. The city's New Zealand Rugby Museum is within walking distance for sports enthusiasts. Palmerston North serves as a base for exploring the wider Manawatu region.

reviews.summary: A well-presented combined cultural facility with good variety. The interactive science exhibits engage children effectively. Free entry makes it an easy stop when in Palmerston North.

pois.name: Tararua Forest Park
pois.short: Rugged forest park spanning the Tararua Range with tramping tracks, alpine crossings, and backcountry huts.
pois.commentary: The Tararua Forest Park covers over 116,000 hectares of the rugged Tararua Range between the Manawatu and Wairarapa, offering some of the North Island's most challenging and rewarding tramping experiences. The range rises from lowland podocarp forest through dense beech forest to exposed alpine tops, with weather conditions that can change rapidly and dramatically. Popular day walks include the Kapakapanui Track and the nature loop at Holdsworth, while multi-day routes like the Southern Crossing traverse the alpine tops between huts. The park's 50-plus DOC backcountry huts range from basic bivvies to well-equipped lodges. The Tararuas are famous among New Zealand trampers for their character-building weather, with the exposed tops experiencing some of the windiest conditions in the country. Experienced trampers value the range for its wilderness feel within easy reach of Wellington and Palmerston North. Access points include Holdsworth, Otaki Forks, and Kapakapanui from the western side.

reviews.summary: A serious tramping destination that rewards experienced walkers with genuine wilderness. The weather is notoriously changeable, so preparation is essential. The backcountry hut network is excellent.

pois.name: Pātea Freezing Works Site
pois.short: Ruins of the freezing works whose 1982 closure inspired a famous protest song.
pois.commentary: The ruins of the Pātea Freezing Works are a poignant reminder of the devastating impact of industrial closures on small New Zealand communities. When the works closed in 1982, it was the town's largest employer, and the resulting unemployment and social disruption inspired the Pātea Māori Club to record 'Poi E', which became New Zealand's biggest-selling single and an enduring cultural anthem. The ruins of the works can be viewed from the road, a stark industrial skeleton against the Tasman coastline. The broader Pātea story encompasses resilience and cultural revival, with the town maintaining its community spirit through sport and arts. Information about the town's history is available at the local museum. Pātea sits on State Highway 3 between Hāwera and Whanganui.

reviews.summary: A moving piece of social and industrial history. The connection to Poi E adds cultural significance. The ruins are stark but powerful.

pois.name: Forgotten World Highway (SH43)
pois.short: NZ's loneliest highway — 150km through hill country, heritage tunnels, and Whangamomona.
pois.commentary: State Highway 43 runs 150 kilometres from Stratford to Taumarunui through some of the most remote hill country in the North Island.

reviews.summary: One of NZ's great drives — remote, atmospheric, and genuinely different. Fill up with fuel before you start.

pois.name: Te Āpiti / Manawatū Gorge Track
pois.short: Walk through the dramatic gorge where the Manawatū River cuts through the Ruahine Range.
pois.commentary: Te Āpiti is a dramatic geological feature where the Manawatū River has cut through the Ruahine Range.

reviews.summary: Dramatic gorge scenery with good bush walking. Native birdlife is abundant.

pois.name: Durie Hill Elevator & Tower
pois.short: Underground tunnel to NZ's only public elevator inside a hill — then climb the war memorial tower for views.
pois.commentary: The Durie Hill Elevator: enter through a tunnel, walk 213 metres into the hill, ride NZ's only underground elevator 66 metres up.

reviews.summary: A unique NZ experience — the tunnel and elevator are quirky and fun. Views from the tower are excellent.

pois.name: Owlcatraz
pois.short: NZ's only owl sanctuary — meet morepork, ruru, and other native birds on guided tours.
pois.commentary: Owlcatraz is New Zealand's only dedicated owl sanctuary, featuring morepork (ruru) and other native birds.

reviews.summary: The night tours are magical — hearing morepork call in the dark is unforgettable.

pois.name: The Esplanade & Wildbase Recovery
pois.short: Beautiful park with world-class Wildbase Recovery wildlife hospital — see injured native birds being rehabilitated.
pois.commentary: The Esplanade features rose gardens, bush walks, and the Wildbase Recovery centre — a public-facing wildlife hospital.

reviews.summary: Wildbase Recovery is fascinating — seeing native birds being rehabilitated is special.

pois.name: Rangitīkei River — White Water Grade 5
pois.short: One of NZ's best white water rafting rivers — Grade 5 rapids through a stunning gorge.
pois.commentary: The Rangitīkei River offers some of the North Island's best white water rafting with Grade 5 rapids.

reviews.summary: Serious white water in a stunning gorge. Not for beginners but experienced rafters will love it.

pois.name: Bushy Park Tarapuruhi Ecosanctuary
pois.short: Predator-free forest sanctuary with a heritage homestead — rich birdlife including North Island robin and kākā.
pois.commentary: Bushy Park is a 100-hectare predator-free ecosanctuary enclosed by a pest-proof fence allowing native birds to thrive.

reviews.summary: The birdlife is incredible — robins hop right up to you. The bush is magnificent ancient forest.

pois.name: Bason Botanic Gardens
pois.short: 25 hectares of landscaped gardens and native bush — a peaceful green retreat on the outskirts of Whanganui.
pois.commentary: Bason Botanic Gardens covers 25 hectares, combining formal gardens, a hothouse collection, and regenerating native bush. The native bush section has been intensively predator-trapped, bringing back bellbirds, kererū, and fantails. Free entry, open daily.

reviews.summary: A lovely, underrated garden with excellent native bush walks. Bird life is noticeably good.

pois.name: Whanganui Journey (Great Walk)
pois.short: One of NZ’s 10 Great Walks — but you paddle it. A 3–5 day canoe or kayak journey down the Whanganui River.
pois.commentary: The Whanganui Journey is unique among Great Walks — it’s a river trip. Over 3–5 days, you paddle 145 km through deep gorges, past ancient bush, and under the Bridge to Nowhere. The river is Te Awa Tupua — a legal person under NZ law.

reviews.summary: A uniquely New Zealand experience — paddling through pristine wilderness on a river with legal personhood.

pois.name: Te Manawa Museum
pois.short: Palmerston North’s combined museum, science centre and art gallery.
pois.commentary: Te Manawa combines a museum, art gallery and science centre under one roof. The museum covers the Manawatū region’s history, with good coverage of Rangiitāne a Manawatū. Free entry.

reviews.summary: A solid regional museum with good interactive science exhibits for kids.

pois.name: Whanganui Victoria Avenue Food Precinct
pois.short: Whanganui's Victoria Avenue café and restaurant precinct — a compact and quality food scene with genuine local character.
pois.commentary: Victoria Avenue is Whanganui's main street and has developed a legitimate food precinct. Yellow House Café remains the flagship — a riverside institution in a faithfully restored heritage building. Stellar Restaurant is the most ambitious dinner option in town. The Saturday morning Whanganui Market at Moutoa Gardens (Pakaitore) is the week's social and culinary event — locally grown produce, artisan bakers, and excellent coffee carts. Whanganui punches above its size for food quality; several Wellington chefs have relocated here in the past decade for better lifestyle at lower cost. The Saturday market is the most authentic expression of the town's food culture.

reviews.summary: Whanganui's food scene is a genuine surprise for first-time visitors. Quality is consistently high. The Saturday market is a highlight. Heritage buildings add atmosphere to the dining experience.

pois.name: The Brew Union, Whanganui
pois.short: Whanganui's craft beer hub — a working brewery and bar serving excellent rotating taps in a heritage Victoria Avenue building.
pois.commentary: The Brew Union occupies a heritage building on Victoria Avenue and brews its beers on-site — rotating taps showcase lagers, pale ales, IPAs, and seasonal specials made with Whanganui water. The venue has a relaxed public bar feel, good bar food including wood-fired pizza, and hosts live music on weekends. Whanganui has an unexpectedly strong craft beer culture for its size, and the Brew Union is its anchor. The brewing equipment is visible through a glass wall from the bar, and staff explain the brewing process on request. The after-work and evening crowd is genuinely local, making this a social experience rather than a tourist operation.

reviews.summary: Excellent craft beer, good food, and a genuine local atmosphere. The brewpub feel is authentic. Live music nights are particularly good. A solid choice for an evening in Whanganui.

pois.name: Ohakune Mountain Road Food and Café Scene
pois.short: Ohakune's ski-town café culture — excellent coffee, hearty mountain food, and the famous carrot-themed local produce.
pois.commentary: Ohakune is New Zealand's most successful ski-resort town food scene — what started as après-ski fuelling has evolved into year-round café culture with genuine quality. The Powderkeg Bar and Grill on the mountain road sets the tone: large portions, good wine list, open fire in winter. The Ohakune Carrot (a giant carrot marks the entrance to town) celebrates the region's market-gardening heritage — Ohakune is New Zealand's carrot capital and local produce appears on every menu. Utopia Café handles breakfast before a mountain day; the Junction Hotel handles dinner. In summer, a thriving biking community has expanded the food demand considerably.

reviews.summary: Ohakune's food scene punches well above its town size. The mountain culture gives it energy year-round. Local carrots and venison appear on most menus — try both.

pois.name: Palmerston North Farmers' Market (The Square)
pois.short: Palmerston North's Saturday farmers' market at The Square — one of the Manawatū's best food experiences.
pois.commentary: The Palmerston North Farmers' Market at Te Marae a Hine / The Square runs every Saturday morning and is the Manawatū's best single showcase of the region's food culture. Over 40 stalls feature local growers, artisan bakers, cheesemakers (including the outstanding Kingsmeade Sheep Milk Cheese), local honey, and craft coffee roasters. The market reflects the Manawatū's role as one of NZ's most productive agricultural regions. Arrive by 9am for the best selection. The Square itself is one of the most pleasant public spaces in the lower North Island — the Saturday market transforms it into a genuine community hub.

reviews.summary: An excellent farmers' market in a beautiful setting. The artisan food quality is high. Kingsmeade cheeses are outstanding. A Saturday morning at The Square is the best way to experience Palmerston North's community spirit.

pois.name: Rangitikei Brewing Co., Bulls
pois.short: Bulls' craft brewery — small-batch beers brewed with Rangitikei water and local provenance at the heart of the operation.
pois.commentary: Rangitikei Brewing Co. operates from Bulls — the famous 'Can't Stop Won't Stop' pun town on SH1 — and has built a reputation for well-crafted small-batch beers. The brewery bar opens Thursday to Sunday and serves tasting paddles alongside a simple food menu featuring local beef, lamb, and seasonal produce. Bulls is a natural stop on SH1 between Wellington and Palmerston North, and the brewery gives it a reason to slow down. The Rangitikei River, 10 minutes west, provides exceptional jet boating and fly fishing — the brewery is the natural end-of-day reward.

reviews.summary: Good beer in a friendly local atmosphere. The pun-town context adds to the charm. A natural and enjoyable SH1 stop that deserves more visitors.

pois.name: Palmerston North Dining — George Street and The Square
pois.short: Palmerston North's diverse and quality dining scene — George Street and The Square offer everything from Vietnamese to contemporary NZ cuisine.
pois.commentary: Palmerston North's status as a university city (Massey University) and agricultural services hub has created a genuinely diverse food scene. The Square precinct and George Street have the highest concentration of quality dining — Déjà Vu (contemporary NZ), the Deco Restaurant (heritage building), a strong Japanese selection, and Vietnamese cuisine that reflects the university's diverse student population. George Street's café strip handles morning rituals reliably; The Square's restaurants handle the evening work with ambition. The food scene is consistently underestimated by passing travellers on the SH1 corridor.

reviews.summary: Palmerston North's food scene is consistently underestimated by passing travellers. The diversity is real and the quality is high. George Street cafes are a morning highlight.

pois.name: Pohangina Valley Wine and Orchard Trail
pois.short: The Pohangina Valley east of Palmerston North has boutique wineries and orchards open to visitors on the road to the Ruahine Range.
pois.commentary: The Pohangina Valley is a beautiful pastoral valley running east from the Manawatū plains toward the Ruahine Range foothills. Escarpment Winery is among the Manawatū's most awarded — concentrated pinot noir and chardonnay from a privileged site with views to the range. Local apple and pear orchards with farm shops, market gardeners selling direct, and a growing number of artisan producers have established in the valley over 20 years. The valley road is one of the most scenic in the region — narrow, tree-lined, with the Pohangina River visible through farmland. The combination of excellent wine, farm produce, and genuine rural character rewards visitors willing to leave SH2.

reviews.summary: A hidden gem. The valley scenery is beautiful and the Escarpment wines are exceptional. Most visitors drive straight past on SH2 — their loss.

pois.name: Feilding Saleyards and Friday Country Market
pois.short: New Zealand's largest livestock market on Friday mornings — then a country café and farmers' market in NZ's most beautiful small town.
pois.commentary: Feilding's Friday saleyards are the largest livestock saleyards in New Zealand by throughput — up to 25,000 sheep and cattle sold in a morning. The Friday market draws farmers from across the Manawatū and the atmosphere is pure rural New Zealand. The adjacent Friday Country Market (produce, baking, plants) and the saleyards café serve the farming crowd from 7am. Guided saleyards tours run at 11am on Fridays — one of the most authentic agricultural tourism experiences in the country. Feilding holds New Zealand's 'Most Beautiful Small Town' award and the main street is worth a walk after the market.

reviews.summary: An utterly authentic slice of rural New Zealand. The saleyards tour is one of the most unique tourism experiences in the Manawatū. The Friday market is excellent. Come hungry — the café is very good.

pois.name: Holdsworth–Jumbo Ridge, Tararua Forest Park
pois.short: One of the Tararua Range's finest ridge walks — spectacular views, diverse beech forest, and classic NZ backcountry.
pois.commentary: The Holdsworth area is the most accessible entry point to the Tararua Forest Park — one of the largest and wildest areas of native forest in the lower North Island. The Holdsworth–Jumbo Ridge circuit (two days, one hut) gives the full Tararua experience: dense red and silver beech forest giving way to subalpine tussock, ridge-line walking with views from the Tasman to the Pacific on clear days, and the characteristically changeable Tararua weather. Day walkers can access the Powell Hut track from Holdsworth. Kaka, robins, and whio (blue duck) have been recorded in the upper valley. Hut bookings are recommended in summer.

reviews.summary: The Tararua Range is New Zealand's hidden tramping gem. The Holdsworth entry is excellent. The weather can change rapidly — always check the forecast and carry full gear regardless of conditions.

pois.name: Manawatū Gorge — Te Āpiti Track
pois.short: The Manawatū Gorge cuts through the Tararua Range — a remarkable geological feature with a 3km bush walk and excellent birdlife.
pois.commentary: The Manawatū Gorge is one of New Zealand's most unusual geological features — the Manawatū River cuts directly through the Tararua Range rather than going around it, a phenomenon known as antecedent drainage. The gorge walk follows the true right bank for 3km through dense native bush, with the river visible below and the Tararua ridge rising steeply above. The Te Āpiti wind farm's turbines crown the ridge — a striking combination of natural and engineered landscape. Kākā and bellbird are regularly heard. The round-trip walk takes about three hours. Check for track closures before visiting as slips occasionally affect access.

reviews.summary: A genuinely impressive geological feature. The walk is rewarding and the birdlife is excellent. The wind turbines on the ridge add unexpected visual drama. Check for track closures before visiting.

pois.name: Rangitikei River Gorge and Jet Boating
pois.short: The Rangitikei cuts a spectacular limestone gorge through central NI country — jet boating, fly fishing, and clifftop walks.
pois.commentary: The Rangitikei River is one of the least commercialised of the central North Island's major rivers — clean, with a significant limestone gorge section between Mangaweka and Taihape. Adventure Rangitikei operates jet boat tours up the gorge from Mangaweka, carving between 20m limestone bluffs covered in native bush. The old Mangaweka suspension bridge beside the new road bridge is the most photographed spot in the gorge. The Rangitikei is rated among NZ's top ten salmon and trout fishing rivers; guides operate multi-day trips. The river valley through Utiku and Moawhango passes some of the most unspoiled pastoral landscape in the Manawatū.

reviews.summary: The Rangitikei gorge is a revelation. The jet boat experience is excellent and the fly fishing is world-class. The river feels genuinely wild and unspoiled compared to more touristed waterways.

pois.name: Bushy Park Tarapuruhi Wildlife Sanctuary
pois.short: A 100-hectare pest-free bush sanctuary 25 minutes from Whanganui — kiwi, kaka, and threatened native birds in restored kahikatea forest.
pois.commentary: Bushy Park Tarapuruhi is managed by Forest and Bird and is one of the few pest-free mainland sanctuaries in the lower North Island. The predator-proof fence encloses 100 hectares of kahikatea and tawa forest with a strong collection of rare native birds: North Island brown kiwi (daytime walks as well as night tours), kaka, North Island robin, whiteheads, and long-tailed bats. The historic homestead (1905, Category I heritage building) is available for accommodation. Night kiwi tours run regularly and should be booked in advance. The sanctuary is 25 minutes from Whanganui and easily combined with the Whanganui River Road.

reviews.summary: The kiwi night tours are exceptional — reliable sightings in a beautiful bush setting. The daytime birdlife is equally impressive. The historic homestead adds a wonderful accommodation option. Highly recommended.

pois.name: Mt Ruapehu — Whakapapa Walking Tracks
pois.short: Walking tracks from Whakapapa Village on Mt Ruapehu — volcanic landscapes, crater lakes, and alpine gardens.
pois.commentary: While the Tongariro Alpine Crossing gets most of the attention, Mt Ruapehu's flanks offer excellent walking tracks with significantly fewer crowds. The Taranaki Falls Track from Whakapapa Village (2 hours return) drops through native bush and tussock to a 20m waterfall over volcanic boulders. The Silica Rapids Track (3 hours return) visits mineral-rich silica terraces. The Tama Lakes (5 hours return) give access to the dramatic crater lake pair on Ruapehu's eastern side. All tracks are well maintained by DOC. The volcanic landscape — geothermal steam vents, lava flows, and alpine herbfields — is unlike anywhere else in New Zealand.

reviews.summary: The Ruapehu tracks are superb alternatives to the crowded Crossing. The volcanic landscape is extraordinary. The Taranaki Falls track is accessible to all fitness levels. Start early for the best weather window.

pois.name: Palmerston North Esplanade Gardens and Rose Collection
pois.short: Palmerston North's treasured riverside gardens — 120 years old, over 2,000 rose bushes, and a riverside walkway.
pois.commentary: The Palmerston North Esplanade is one of the oldest and most loved public parks in the Manawatū — 27 hectares of formal gardens, a rose collection (over 2,000 bushes of 300 varieties, best in December), children's play areas, and a miniature railway. The park borders the Manawatū River and connects to the Riverside Walkway extending 10km upstream toward the gorge. The formal rose garden was established in 1906 and the spring and summer displays attract visitors from across the region. Free entry; a café operates on site. The park is extensively used for concerts and food festivals through summer.

reviews.summary: A well-maintained and beautifully established park. The rose garden in December is exceptional. Good family facilities and a pleasant café. The riverside walks extend the experience significantly.

pois.name: Foxton Beach and Manawatū Estuary (Godwit Habitat)
pois.short: The Manawatū Estuary at Foxton — one of NZ's most important wader habitats, famous for bar-tailed godwit gatherings.
pois.commentary: The Manawatū Estuary at Foxton Beach is one of the most significant waterbird habitats in the lower North Island. Bar-tailed godwits arrive here after their remarkable 11,000km non-stop flight from Alaska each September and stay through March — the estuary holds several thousand birds during peak season. The godwits departing in March make the longest non-stop migratory flight of any bird. Red knots, wrybills, lesser knots, and shovelers are regularly present. The Foxton Beach Wetland Loop (2km boardwalk) gives good estuary access; high tide concentrates birds at the roost near the carpark. Binoculars are essential.

reviews.summary: A world-class wader destination. The godwit gatherings in September and March are remarkable spectacles. The boardwalk gives excellent viewing access. Take binoculars — this is serious birdwatching country.

pois.name: Whanganui — Virginia Lake and Kowhai Park
pois.short: Virginia Lake and its bird sanctuary walkway — Whanganui's inner-city nature reserve with tui, bellbird, and native waterbirds.
pois.commentary: Virginia Lake sits just north of Whanganui city centre and has a 2km walking circuit around its shores, passing through an old-growth kowhai grove (spectacular in August-September when in flower) and a restored wetland and bird sanctuary. Tui and bellbirds are resident in the kowhai through winter and spring; paradise shelduck, grey teal, and white-faced heron frequent the lake margins. The lake was developed as a public park in the 1890s and the heritage band rotunda, fernery, and formal plantings are well-maintained. The adjacent Kowhai Park has a children's playground, making this a natural family stop in Whanganui.

reviews.summary: A lovely inner-city escape. The kowhai in flower is spectacular and the tui presence is excellent. Well-maintained and accessible. A good 90-minute circuit for families and birdwatchers.

pois.name: Totara Reserve, Pohangina Valley
pois.short: The best lowland totara forest remaining in the Manawatū — a 50-hectare DOC reserve with excellent walking tracks and river access.
pois.commentary: Totara Reserve in the Pohangina Valley protects 50 hectares of the most significant lowland totara forest remaining in the Manawatū. Totara — the building and waka timber that was the technological foundation of Māori civilisation — was cleared almost entirely from the plains after European settlement. This grove was protected in 1900 and now supports a rich secondary forest. Walking tracks run 4km alongside the Pohangina River, with a series of river crossings and pools popular for summer swimming. Kererū, kaka, long-tailed bats, and native eels are present. The reserve is 30 minutes from Palmerston North and remains deeply undervisited.

reviews.summary: A beautiful and historically significant forest. The combination of river swimming and native bush is excellent in summer. The track is well-maintained. One of Manawatū's best natural areas.

pois.name: Mt Ruapehu — Alpine Volcanic Environment (Whakapapa and Turoa)
pois.short: Mt Ruapehu's ski fields sit on an active volcano — one of the world's most extraordinary natural alpine environments.
pois.commentary: Mt Ruapehu's two ski fields — Whakapapa (the North Island's largest) and Turoa (the south face, accessed from Ohakune) — together operate on the flanks of an active volcano whose crater lake has erupted three times since 1995. The natural environment is extraordinary: steam vents alongside ski runs, volcanic rock formations as natural features, and the constant reminder that this mountain is geologically alive. In summer, the ski fields become walking and mountain biking terrain with spectacular alpine views. The DOC Visitor Centre at Whakapapa provides volcanic monitoring information and the alpine flora is diverse and beautiful in season.

reviews.summary: Skiing on an active volcano is a uniquely New Zealand experience. The summer mountain walks are excellent. The volcanic landscape is extraordinary and the views on clear days are among the finest in the North Island.

pois.name: Whanganui River Gorge — Jet Boat to Bridge to Nowhere
pois.short: Jet up the Whanganui River gorge through remote bush to the Bridge to Nowhere — the most dramatic way to experience Te Awa Tupua.
pois.commentary: Jet boat operators run trips from Pipiriki into the upper Whanganui River gorge, where the river narrows between steep bush-clad hills and the road ends. Whanganui River Adventures runs trips up through the gorge to the Bridge to Nowhere landing — a 1936 structure built for a farming settlement that was abandoned after the 1942 earthquake, leaving the bridge standing in pristine bush with no road on either side. The jet boat approach through the deep gorge and tall native bush is dramatic, and allows access without a multi-day canoe trip. Te Awa Tupua — the Whanganui River as a legal person — gives the experience an additional layer of meaning.

reviews.summary: The most dramatic way to experience the Whanganui River gorge. The Bridge to Nowhere approach by jet boat is genuinely spectacular. The guides are knowledgeable about river ecology and cultural significance.

pois.name: Taihape Gumboot / Big Gumboot
pois.short: Self-proclaimed Gumboot Capital of NZ with giant gumboot sculpture and annual throwing contest.
pois.commentary: Taihape proudly declares itself the Gumboot Capital of New Zealand, celebrating its rural farming identity with a corrugated iron giant gumboot sculpture on the main street. The town hosts the annual Gumboot Day festival where competitors hurl gumboots for distance and accuracy in a quintessentially Kiwi competition. Beyond the quirky attraction, Taihape is the gateway to the Rangitikei River, which offers world-class white-water rafting through spectacular gorge scenery. The main street retains charming heritage buildings and several good cafes.

reviews.summary: A fun, quick photo stop. The town is more charming than expected and the surrounding Rangitikei country is beautiful.

pois.name: Rangitikei River White Water Rafting
pois.short: Grade 3-5 rafting through spectacular deep gorges of the Rangitikei River.
pois.commentary: The Rangitikei River cuts through some of the deepest gorges in the North Island, creating world-class white-water rafting through towering papa cliff walls. Trips range from family-friendly Grade 2-3 sections to the adrenaline-pumping Grade 5 rapids of the upper gorge. The river's clear blue-green water runs through bush-clad valleys with virtually no signs of human habitation, giving a real wilderness feel despite being only two hours from Wellington or Palmerston North. Several operators run half-day and full-day trips from Mangaweka or Taihape.

reviews.summary: Stunning gorge scenery and exciting rapids. The remoteness of the canyon adds to the adventure. One of NZ's best-kept rafting secrets.

pois.name: Mangaweka / Big Aeroplane
pois.short: Tiny village famous for its DC-3 aeroplane on the roadside.
pois.commentary: Mangaweka is a blink-and-miss-it village on State Highway 1 between Taihape and Hunterville, but its DC-3 aeroplane parked beside the road makes it one of the most photographed spots in the Manawatu-Rangitikei. The decommissioned aircraft once served as a fish-and-chip shop and information centre, and while the plane is no longer serving food, it remains an iconic roadside attraction. The village itself has an appealing time-capsule quality with heritage buildings that hint at its busier past. Mangaweka is also the main put-in point for Rangitikei River rafting and kayaking trips.

reviews.summary: A quick, fun photo stop. The plane is quirky and photogenic. The village has a pleasant abandoned charm.

pois.name: Manawatu Gorge Track / Te Apiti
pois.short: Walk through a dramatic wind gap where the Manawatu River cuts through the Tararua Range.
pois.commentary: The Manawatu Gorge is one of New Zealand's geological curiosities — a river that flows right through a mountain range rather than around it, creating a dramatic wind gap between the Tararua and Ruahine Ranges. Te Apiti walking and cycling tracks offer access to native bush in the gorge, with kahikatea, rimu, and totara forest that is home to kereru, tui, and bellbirds. The adjacent Te Apiti wind farm's massive turbines are visible from the track, creating an interesting juxtaposition of ancient bush and modern renewable energy. Multiple track options range from 30-minute loops to longer traverses.

reviews.summary: Surprisingly beautiful gorge walk with excellent native bush. The river cutting through mountains is a geological marvel.

pois.name: Royal Wanganui Opera House
pois.short: Beautifully restored 1899 opera house with a rare working Wurlitzer organ.
pois.commentary: The Royal Wanganui Opera House is one of New Zealand's finest heritage theatres, opened in 1899 and impeccably restored to its Edwardian grandeur. The jewel of the building is its rare Wurlitzer theatre organ, one of only a handful of working Wurlitzers remaining in concert venues worldwide. Saturday organ recitals are a Whanganui institution and offer a chance to hear this magnificent instrument. The 830-seat auditorium hosts a year-round programme of theatre, music, comedy, and dance. Guided tours take in the backstage areas, the organ chambers, and the ornate ceiling and plasterwork.

reviews.summary: A beautifully maintained heritage theatre with real character. The Wurlitzer organ is extraordinary. Check the events programme.

pois.name: Paddle Steamer Waimarie
pois.short: Cruise the Whanganui River on NZ's only coal-fired paddle steamer, salvaged from the riverbed.
pois.commentary: The Paddle Steamer Waimarie is New Zealand's only operating coal-fired paddle steamer, with an extraordinary resurrection story. Built in London in 1899, she served on the Whanganui River until 1952, when she sank at her berth and lay submerged for nearly 50 years. A community effort salvaged and restored her, relaunching in 2000. Today, she offers cruises on the Whanganui River, the gentle throb of her steam engine and the slap of paddle wheels evoking the golden age of river transport. The river itself is deeply significant — Te Awa Tupua, the Whanganui River, was granted legal personhood in 2017, recognising its importance to Whanganui iwi.

reviews.summary: A magnificent heritage vessel with a remarkable rescue story. The river cruise is peaceful and scenic. A unique New Zealand experience.

pois.name: New Zealand Glassworks
pois.short: National centre for glass art with working studios, exhibitions, and blow-your-own experiences.
pois.commentary: New Zealand Glassworks is the national centre for contemporary glass art, located in a former Whanganui warehouse. The facility provides working studios for resident and visiting glass artists, hosts exhibitions, and offers blow-your-own-glass experiences where visitors create their own glass piece under guidance. Whanganui has become New Zealand's glass art capital, with a concentration of glass artists attracted by the Glassworks facilities and the city's creative community. The gallery showcases extraordinary works by New Zealand's leading glass artists. Watching skilled artists shape molten glass is mesmerising, and the blow-your-own sessions are surprisingly accessible for beginners.

reviews.summary: Fascinating insight into glass art. The blow-your-own experience is brilliant fun. Whanganui's creative scene is genuine.

pois.name: Bushy Park Tarapuruhi Sanctuary
pois.short: Predator-fenced forest sanctuary with rare birds including North Island brown kiwi.
pois.commentary: Bushy Park Tarapuruhi is a predator-fenced wildlife sanctuary protecting 100 hectares of ancient lowland forest just 15 minutes from Whanganui. Inside the pest-proof fence, native birds thrive — North Island brown kiwi, kererū, tūī, bellbirds, and kākā are all present in numbers rarely seen elsewhere. The forest itself is magnificent, with towering rātā, rimu, and kamahi creating a dense, bird-filled canopy. Walking tracks loop through the forest, and the dawn chorus is exceptional. The sanctuary demonstrates what New Zealand's forests can be when predators are removed. A homestead on site offers accommodation for those wanting the full dawn-to-dusk experience.

reviews.summary: Extraordinary birdlife in a beautiful forest setting. The dawn chorus is one of the best in the North Island. Conservation success story.

pois.name: Forgotten World Adventures Rail Cart
pois.short: Self-drive rail carts through tunnels and gorges on an abandoned railway into the Forgotten World.
pois.commentary: Forgotten World Adventures offers an extraordinary experience — self-driving rail carts along a 32km section of abandoned railway from Whangamomona, through 20 tunnels (including the 1.6km Moki Tunnel), across bridges, and through the spectacular Tangarakau Gorge into the remote 'Forgotten World' of inland Taranaki. The custom-built rail carts travel on the original railway tracks through landscape so rugged and remote that the road bypassed it entirely. The experience passes through dense bush, dramatic gorges, and into the abandoned Tangarakau settlement. No railway experience needed — the carts are simple to operate. It's arguably one of New Zealand's most unique tourism experiences.

reviews.summary: Absolutely extraordinary experience through spectacular scenery. The tunnels are thrilling. One of NZ's most unique activities.

pois.name: Whanganui Regional Museum
pois.short: Outstanding collection of Māori taonga and Gottfried Lindauer portraits of 19th-century Māori leaders.
pois.commentary: The Whanganui Regional Museum holds one of New Zealand's most significant collections of Māori taonga (treasures), including carvings, waka, weapons, and personal adornments. The museum's crown jewels are the Gottfried Lindauer portraits — beautifully detailed oil paintings of Māori leaders from the late 1800s that are both artistically significant and historically invaluable as records of prominent rangatira. The museum's Māori Court displays are among the best in the country. The wider collection covers natural history and colonial settlement. The museum is a reminder that Whanganui's cultural significance far exceeds its population size.

reviews.summary: Outstanding Māori collection and the Lindauer portraits are magnificent. One of NZ's best regional museums. Well worth a visit.

pois.name: Bridge to Nowhere — Whanganui River
pois.short: Concrete bridge in remote bush built for settlers who later abandoned the area — a monument to failed ambition.
pois.commentary: The Bridge to Nowhere is one of New Zealand's most evocative historic sites — a large concrete bridge spanning the Mangapurua Stream deep in the Whanganui National Park, built in 1936 to serve farming families who had settled the remote Mangapurua Valley after World War I. The settlement failed within decades — the land was too steep, too isolated, and too unforgiving — and the families left. The bush reclaimed the farms, but the bridge endures, standing incongruously in dense forest with no road at either end. Access is by jet boat up the Whanganui River followed by a 40-minute bush walk. The bridge is a powerful monument to ambition, resilience, and ultimately, nature's victory.

reviews.summary: Profoundly evocative historic site. The bush setting amplifies the bridge's lonely presence. The jet boat ride is thrilling.

pois.name: Virginia Lake & Winter Gardens
pois.short: Beautiful heritage lake with illuminated fountain, winter gardens, and bird aviaries.
pois.commentary: Virginia Lake is Whanganui's premier public park — a picturesque lake surrounded by mature trees, with an illuminated fountain, winter gardens, bird aviaries, and the adjacent Kowhai Park children's playground. The lake loop walk (1.5km) is a favourite local exercise route. The Winter Gardens house subtropical and native plants in a heritage greenhouse setting. The bird aviaries hold a collection of native and exotic species. The Higginbottom Fountain in the lake centre is illuminated at night with coloured lights. The park has a timeless, well-loved quality that reflects Whanganui's pride in its public spaces. The café overlooking the lake is popular.

reviews.summary: A lovely heritage park with genuine charm. The illuminated fountain is beautiful at night. Good café. Popular with locals.

pois.name: Te Manawa Museum & Science Centre
pois.short: Combined museum, art gallery, and interactive science centre in Palmerston North.
pois.commentary: Te Manawa is a combined museum, art gallery, and interactive science centre in Palmerston North — three institutions under one roof. The museum section covers Manawatū regional history and Māori heritage. The art gallery holds a significant collection of New Zealand and international art. The science centre, designed for families, features interactive exhibits exploring physics, technology, and natural science. The integration of art, history, and science creates a diverse experience that appeals to different interests. Rotating exhibitions keep return visits fresh. It's one of the North Island's better combined cultural facilities and a good reason to pause in Palmerston North.

reviews.summary: Good combined museum with something for everyone. The interactive science section is great for kids. Free entry.

pois.name: Whanganui River Road
pois.short: 79km remote road following the Whanganui River through gorges, past marae, and into NZ's heartland.
pois.commentary: The Whanganui River Road is one of New Zealand's most atmospheric drives — 79km of winding, mostly unsealed road following the Whanganui River upstream from Whanganui to Pipiriki. The road passes through deep gorges, past small Māori communities and marae, through bush and farmland, with the river as constant companion. Historic flour mills, suspension bridges, and the haunting Ōyster Shell Church at Jerusalem (Hiruhārama) — where poet James K. Baxter established his commune in the 1960s — punctuate the journey. The road is narrow and slow (allow 2-3 hours each way) but the remoteness and beauty are profound. This is the New Zealand heartland at its most authentic.

reviews.summary: One of NZ's most atmospheric drives. Remote, beautiful, and culturally rich. Take your time and stop often.

pois.name: New Zealand Glassworks / Te Whare Tūhua o Te Ao
pois.short: National centre for glass art with live demonstrations and gallery.
pois.commentary: New Zealand Glassworks is the country's only dedicated centre for glass art, located in the heart of Whanganui's creative quarter. The facility houses professional glass-blowing studios, a gallery, and offers public demonstrations where visitors can watch artists create intricate works in molten glass. Whanganui's designation as New Zealand's first UNESCO City of Design is partly due to institutions like this. The gallery showcases stunning works by resident and visiting artists, with pieces available for purchase. Glassblowing workshops for beginners are available by booking. The centre is part of Whanganui's vibrant creative scene, which includes pottery, painting, and sculpture studios in the surrounding streets. A unique cultural experience.

reviews.summary: Visitors are fascinated by the live glassblowing demonstrations. The gallery has beautiful, unique pieces. A highlight of Whanganui's creative scene.

pois.name: Putiki Church / St Paul's Memorial Church
pois.short: Beautifully decorated Māori church with intricate interior panels.
pois.commentary: St Paul's Memorial Church at Putiki is one of Whanganui's cultural treasures, a modest exterior concealing an extraordinary interior covered in traditional Māori tukutuku panels, kōwhaiwhai patterns, and carved pou. The church was built in 1937 and the interior decorations tell the stories of local iwi and their relationship with the Whanganui River. The contrast between the plain wooden exterior and the richly decorated interior is remarkable. Visitors are welcome but should enter respectfully and seek permission if possible. The adjacent Putiki Marae is one of the oldest in the country. Photography may be restricted inside. Located across the river from the city centre, it's easily reached by car or a walk across the city bridge.

reviews.summary: Visitors are amazed by the stunning interior artwork. Often described as a hidden gem of Whanganui. Respectful visiting is important.

pois.name: Manawatū Gorge Tawa Loop Track
pois.short: Best short walk in the region through native forest above the gorge.
pois.commentary: The Tawa Loop Track in the Manawatū Gorge is widely considered the best short walk in the Whanganui-Manawatū region. The 4km return track climbs through beautiful tawa, rimu, and northern rātā forest to Tom's Lookout, which offers panoramic views across Palmerston North, the Tararua Range, and the Manawatū River carving through the gorge below. The track is well-maintained with steps and boardwalks in steep sections. Native birdlife is abundant, with kererū, tūī, and bellbirds commonly seen and heard. The gorge is ecologically significant as a wildlife corridor connecting the Ruahine and Tararua Ranges. Allow 1-1.5 hours for the loop. Located on SH3 between Woodville and Ashhurst. Free entry.

reviews.summary: Walkers enjoy the beautiful native bush and the rewarding viewpoint. Good birdlife throughout. An easily accessible taste of NZ native forest.

pois.name: Whanganui River Traders Market
pois.short: Saturday riverside market with local produce, crafts, and street food.
pois.commentary: The Whanganui River Traders Market is a vibrant Saturday morning market held on the banks of the Whanganui River, bringing together local food producers, artisan crafters, and street food vendors. The market has been a community institution for over 20 years, offering everything from fresh organic vegetables and artisan bread to handmade jewellery and pottery. Food highlights include The Big Moose for smoked BBQ, fresh-baked pastries, and excellent coffee from local roasters. The riverside setting adds atmosphere, and local musicians often provide live entertainment. The market runs every Saturday morning from around 8:30am to 12:30pm, rain or shine. It's the best way to sample Whanganui's creative and culinary culture in one stop.

reviews.summary: Locals and visitors enjoy the friendly atmosphere and quality local produce. The food stalls are excellent. A great Saturday morning experience.

pois.name: Ōhakune Old Coach Road
pois.short: A heritage cycle and walking trail between Ōhakune and Horopito — cobblestone roads, bush tunnels and a magnificent 17-metre viaduct.
pois.commentary: The Old Coach Road follows the original 1906 route that connected Ōhakune to the Main Trunk Railway, threading through native bush, across cobblestone surfaces, and over the spectacular Hapuawhenua Viaduct — a graceful steel structure spanning a bush-filled gorge. The trail is 15 km one way and is best done on a mountain bike or e-bike, with shuttle services running between the two ends. The engineering heritage — viaducts, cuttings, retaining walls — is impressive, and the bush sections are atmospheric, with moss-draped trees forming natural tunnels. It's one of the North Island's best half-day cycling experiences.

reviews.summary: A superb cycling trail with varied terrain. The viaduct is a highlight. The bush sections are beautiful. Shuttle services make logistics easy. Some cobblestone sections are rough on bikes.

pois.name: Jerusalem / Hiruhārama — James K Baxter Heritage
pois.short: The riverside settlement where poet James K Baxter established his famous commune in 1969 — a pilgrimage for New Zealand literature lovers.
pois.commentary: Jerusalem (Hiruhārama) is a tiny settlement on the Whanganui River Road where New Zealand's most celebrated poet, James K Baxter, established a commune in 1969. Baxter lived here until his death in 1972, writing some of his finest poetry and advocating for a simpler, more communal life. The settlement includes the historic Catholic mission and convent established by Mother Aubert in the 1880s. Today it's a place of pilgrimage for literature lovers — Baxter's grave is on the hillside above the river. The setting is achingly beautiful: the river curving through steep bush-clad hills, with the sounds of birdsong and flowing water.

reviews.summary: A moving literary and historical site. The setting is beautiful. Baxter's grave is peaceful. The convent heritage adds depth. The river road drive is an experience in itself.

pois.name: Whanganui Arts & Glass Quarter
pois.short: A thriving arts precinct in heritage buildings — Whanganui has attracted artists with cheap studio space and is now one of NZ's most creative small cities.
pois.commentary: Whanganui has quietly become one of New Zealand's most vibrant arts communities, attracting painters, glass artists, sculptors and writers with affordable studio space in atmospheric heritage buildings. The creative quarter centres on Taupo Quay and the riverfront, with highlights including the New Zealand Glassworks (the country's only public-access glass-blowing facility), the Sarjeant Gallery (a magnificent 1919 building), and numerous independent galleries and studios. The Whanganui glass art scene is internationally recognised, and visitors can watch glass-blowers at work and purchase directly from the artists. Open Studios events run periodically.

reviews.summary: An underrated arts destination with genuine creative energy. The Glassworks is fascinating. The heritage buildings are beautiful. Artists are welcoming. A pleasant surprise for most visitors.

pois.name: Ōhakune — Carrot Capital & Mountain Town
pois.short: A mountain town with a giant carrot, excellent restaurants, and gateway status to Tongariro — quirky Kiwi character at altitude.
pois.commentary: Ōhakune sits at the southern foot of Mount Ruapehu and serves as the main gateway to the Tongariro National Park from the south. The town is famous for two things: the 7.5-metre giant carrot on its main street (celebrating the surrounding vegetable-growing industry) and its transformation from sleepy railway town to ski-season party town. Outside winter, Ōhakune is an excellent base for walking, mountain biking and exploring the national park. The restaurant scene has improved dramatically in recent years, with several excellent options punching well above the town's weight. The giant carrot photograph is obligatory.

reviews.summary: A fun mountain town with real character. The giant carrot is a classic photo stop. Good restaurants for a small town. Excellent base for Tongariro. Quiet outside ski season but pleasant year-round.

pois.name: Ngā Puna o Tāne — Palmerston North Bush Walks
pois.short: Native bush reserve with walking tracks through lowland podocarp forest in the city.
pois.commentary: Adjacent to Victoria Esplanade, this native bush reserve contains some of the last remaining lowland podocarp forest in the Manawatū. Tracks wind through tōtara, mataī, and kahikatea trees with excellent birdlife including kererū, tūī, and bellbirds.

reviews.summary: Surprising pocket of old-growth forest right in the city.

pois.name: Te Āpiti — Manawatū Gorge Lookout
pois.short: Dramatic gorge lookout with views of wind turbines against the Tararua Ranges.
pois.commentary: Te Āpiti offers panoramic views of the Manawatū Gorge where the river cuts through the Tararua and Ruahine ranges. The viewing platform overlooks the wind farm turbines spinning against the mountain backdrop — a striking blend of natural landscape and renewable energy.

reviews.summary: Great views of the gorge and wind farm — a unique landscape.

pois.name: Bushy Park Homestead & Sanctuary
pois.short: Pest-free forest sanctuary with an Edwardian homestead and rare native birdlife.
pois.commentary: Bushy Park Tarapuruhi is a 99-hectare predator-fenced forest sanctuary surrounding an elegant 1906 Edwardian homestead. The pest-free bush is home to hihi, North Island robin, and kākā, while the homestead offers B&B accommodation — conservation and heritage combined.

reviews.summary: Beautiful sanctuary — the birdlife is extraordinary.

pois.name: Feilding Saleyards & Heritage Precinct
pois.short: New Zealand's largest livestock saleyards in the country's most beautiful small town.
pois.commentary: Feilding hosts New Zealand's largest livestock sale every Friday, with thousands of sheep and cattle traded in a spectacle of rural commerce. The town has repeatedly won NZ's Most Beautiful Small Town award, with a heritage main street of well-preserved Edwardian buildings.

reviews.summary: Authentic rural NZ experience — the Friday sales are a spectacle.

pois.name: Vinegar Hill — Rangitīkei River Gorge
pois.short: Dramatic river gorge with cliff jumping and swimming below white papa cliffs.
pois.commentary: Vinegar Hill on the Rangitīkei River is a local swimming and cliff jumping spot where the river has carved through white papa cliffs. The turquoise-green water below the cliffs makes for dramatic scenery and brave swimmers launch from the ledges above.

reviews.summary: Stunning gorge — the cliff jumping is not for the faint-hearted.

pois.name: Rangiwahia Hut Track
pois.short: A well-graded Ruahine Forest Park tramp to a bushline hut and open tops on the Whanahuia Range.
pois.commentary: Rangiwahia Hut Track gives Manawatū-Whanganui a strong, approachable Ruahine story. DOC describes a well-graded track climbing from the road end through red beech forest, across an arched bridge over the Mangahuia Stream and up toward Rangiwahia Hut near the bushline. It is often used as an overnight tramp for newer trampers and families with capable children, but that does not make the tops tame. Weather, slips, hut facilities and daylight still need checking. On a clear day, the reward is that classic lower North Island mountain feeling: forest below, tussock above and long views toward bigger volcanoes and ranges.

DOC describes a well-graded track climbing from the road end through red beech forest, across an arched bridge over the Mangahuia Stream and up toward Rangiwahia Hut near the bushline. It is often used as an overnight tramp for newer trampers and families with capable children, but that does not make the tops tame. Weather, slips, hut facilities and daylight still need checking. On a clear day, the reward is that classic lower North Island mountain feeling: forest below, tussock above and long views toward bigger volcanoes and ranges.

reviews.summary: A highly regarded accessible Ruahine tramp with hut, forest and tops; visitors stress weather checks and normal backcountry preparation.
